metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State in the What section (IAM subsection) that the `aws_iam_role_policy` Terraform resource label must be `opcert-admin-invoke` (to match the AC1 jq assertion `.[0].name == "opcert-admin-invoke"`), or change the jq to check the IAM policy name attribute `.values.name` instead of the resource label `.name`.
findings.finding: The jq proving command in AC1 asserts `.[0].name == "opcert-admin-invoke"`, where `.name` is the Terraform resource label (from `terraform show -json`'s resource-level `.name` field), not the IAM policy name attribute. The What section names only the IAM role (`rebar-opcert-admin`) and describes the inline policy semantics, but never states that the Terraform resource label for the `aws_iam_role_policy` must be `opcert-admin-invoke`. An executor implementing the What section without reading the AC assertion code may choose a different resource label (e.g. `rebar_opcert_admin_inline`) and produce a correctly secured configuration that nonetheless causes the AC1 jq command to exit non-zero.
findings.location: AC1 jq proving command / What section (IAM subsection)
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the What section's IAM bullet: 'The `aws_iam_role_policy` resource is labeled `opcert-admin-invoke` in Terraform (resource label, not only the IAM policy name) so the AC assertion matches.' Alternatively, change the AC1 jq to `.[0].s.Sid == "OpcertAdminInvoke"` or check `.values.name` — whichever is the intended anchor.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the nginx-bootstrap ordering contradiction: describe how `/etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf` is guaranteed to exist before HOST nginx starts (e.g. commit a safe default stub to the repo at that path, or have a systemd drop-in / ExecStartPre create the file before the nginx unit starts), and add a rollback/back-out note covering the nginx config template change.
findings.finding: The nginx config template gains `include /etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf;` (evaluated at nginx startup time), but `/etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf` is created by `materialize-opcert-guard.sh`, which runs only as part of `compose-up.sh`. Since HOST nginx starts as a system service at boot — before `compose-up.sh` runs — a rebooted or freshly provisioned box will fail to start nginx, breaking all existing services (Gerrit, etc.) because the included file does not yet exist. The What section describes both changes (template gains the include; script runs before docker compose up) without acknowledging this ordering contradiction.
findings.location: What — 'Compose' subsection ('infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template … gains both the include /etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf; directive') and boot-orchestrator description ('BEFORE docker compose up … finishes with nginx -s reload')
findings.suggested_fix: Commit a minimal stub `/etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf` (e.g. `map $http_x_opcert_guard $opcert_guard_ok { default 0; }`) into the repo under `infra/nginx/` and ensure it is deployed to the host as part of IaC/bootstrap (e.g. via a Terraform `null_resource` remote-exec or an Ansible task), so nginx can always start. The materialize script then overwrites the file with the real value and reloads nginx. This eliminates the chicken-and-egg problem and gives a safe default (all requests rejected at the guard check until the real value is seeded).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the complete rotation procedure: after `terraform apply -replace=` the operator must also re-run `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` and `nginx -s reload` on the box; add this as an explicit step in the What section or a runbook note.
findings.finding: The plan claims the origin-guard rotation loop is 'closed' by `terraform apply -replace=` (which updates the SSM SecureString and the API Gateway static header), but the nginx check-value at /etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf is only refreshed when materialize-opcert-guard.sh is explicitly re-run. After a guard rotation, API Gateway sends the new guard header but nginx still validates against the old value, 403-ing all requests until the operator manually re-runs the materialize script and reloads nginx. This is a hidden manual step the plan omits.
findings.location: What section — Compose/nginx wiring; origin-guard provisioning path: '…closing the loop'
findings.suggested_fix: In the rotation description, add: 'after `terraform apply -replace=random_password.opcert_origin_guard`, run `infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh` on the box (via SSM Session Manager) to pull the new guard value into /etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf and reload nginx.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Ensure `/etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf` is always present before nginx starts: either pre-create a stub version (with a non-matching default so no requests pass) in the `install-certbot-timer.sh` or a new install script, or use `include /etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf` with an `include` guard pattern, or have `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` create the file on first run during the initial provisioning before certbot/nginx is set up.
findings.finding: Adding `include /etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf;` to `rebar.conf.template` creates a boot-ordering hazard: on a box restart, systemd starts host nginx (which loads rebar.conf including the include directive) BEFORE `compose-up.sh` runs `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` (which creates the file). If the file is absent, nginx fails to load (`include` of a non-existent file is a fatal nginx config error), taking down the entire reverse proxy (including Gerrit). The plan specifies `nginx -s reload` at end of the materialize script but does not address the initial-boot file-absent case.
findings.location: What section — Compose/nginx wiring: 'the script writes `/etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a line to `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` that is also called during initial provisioning (before `systemctl enable --now nginx`), or pre-create the file with a sentinel `map $http_x_opcert_guard $opcert_guard_ok { default 0; }` (no passing value) so nginx loads safely before the real value is seeded.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update AC4 to use the superseded field name `added_at_log_position` (not `added_at_commit`) when pinning the entry in `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml`, matching the authoritative schema from the parent epic's security-finding #5 resolution (comment #7), and confirm the test assertion validates the correct field name.
findings.finding: AC4 references 'with `added_at_commit` per the epic's era protocol' to pin the trusted environment entry in `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml`, but the parent epic's authoritative design resolution (comment #7, 'Security finding #5 folded in') explicitly supersedes the original schema field `added_at_commit`/`revoked_at_commit` (explicit git SHAs on main) with `added_at_log_position`/`revoked_at_log_position` (tickets-branch log positions, stamped by a `rebar trusted-env` helper). The leaf's AC4 schema reference is thus pointing at the revoked field vocabulary — an executor who follows the leaf's AC4 literally will write `added_at_commit` into the YAML, which will be incompatible with readers built to the superseded epic schema.
findings.location: AC4 — `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` pinning requirement; parent epic 6d0d-7580-e18f-48c3 comment #7
findings.suggested_fix: In AC4, replace 'with `added_at_commit` per the epic's era protocol' with 'with `added_at_log_position` (the current tickets-branch tip, stamped by `rebar trusted-env`) per the epic's revised era-validity protocol (security finding #5 resolution)'. Also add the corresponding `revoked_at_log_position: null` field to the pinned entry.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the AC4 test assertion to explicitly validate the era-boundary field name (`added_at_log_position`, NOT `added_at_commit`) in the pinned `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` entry, so the unit test catches a schema-version mismatch before deploy.
findings.finding: The plan references the 'era protocol' as a processing rule that governs how `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` entries are validated at key-validity-check time, but the two conflicting protocol definitions (original 'Trust config & era-validity protocol' in the epic description vs. the later 'Authoritative era anchor' in epic comment #7) are not reconciled or cross-referenced within the leaf plan itself. An executor reading only this leaf cannot determine which protocol is authoritative for the boot-time pinning step (the leaf says 'per the epic's era protocol' with no disambiguation), and the leaf's own test assertion (AC4: 'a test asserts the entry parses and the `env_id` matches') does not verify the era field name at all — making the protocol gap undetectable by the specified test.
findings.location: AC4 (test assertion clause); What section ('pin the environment's public key + `env_id` into `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` per the epic's era protocol')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update leaf AC4 to use `added_at_log_position` (tickets-branch log position, operator-stamped by `rebar trusted-env`) instead of `added_at_commit`, aligning with the parent's post-finding-#5 authoritative era schema.
findings.finding: Leaf AC4 uses `added_at_commit` as the era-boundary field when pinning the deployed environment's public key in `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml`, but the parent's authoritative post-round-6 security-finding-#5 resolution SUPERSEDES the original 'Trust config & era-validity protocol' and renames those fields to `added_at_log_position` / `revoked_at_log_position` (tickets-branch log positions, not explicit git SHAs on main). The leaf's AC4 therefore contradicts the parent's current schema.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC4: '(with `added_at_commit` per the epic's era protocol)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '(with `added_at_commit` per the epic's era protocol)' in AC4 with '(with `added_at_log_position` stamped by `rebar trusted-env` at pin time, per the epic's post-finding-#5 era-at-storage-anchor protocol)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `prevent_destroy = true` to the `lifecycle` block of the `/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key` SSM parameter resource, matching the pattern already used for `aws_ebs_volume.data` and `aws_eip.gerrit` for other irreplaceable resources.
findings.finding: The SSM SecureString `/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key` — explicitly described as the only copy of the Ed25519 private key — has `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` to prevent Terraform from overwriting the operator-seeded key, but the plan specifies NO `prevent_destroy` on this parameter. A `terraform destroy`, a state-level `terraform state rm` followed by re-apply, or accidental module deletion would destroy the SSM parameter and permanently lose the key with no recovery path.
findings.location: What section — SSM Parameter Store SecureString `/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key`; AC1 — 'the key parameter guarded by lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and cite the specific `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` `request_parameters` syntax for injecting a static (Terraform-interpolated) string as a header value in HTTP_PROXY integrations, and confirm it is supported without requiring a mapping-expression wrapper.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that Terraform's `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` resource can inject a static `X-Opcert-Guard` value as a request header parameter (mapping `random_password.result` into the integration's `request_parameters`). For HTTP_PROXY integrations (`integration_type = "HTTP_PROXY"`), API Gateway v2's `request_parameters` supports overrides for headers, querystring, and path — but the specific behavior when the value is a static Terraform-interpolated string (not a `$request.` or `$context.` mapping expression) is a non-obvious capability. The plan does not cite AWS documentation or the Terraform registry to confirm this works as described.
findings.location: What section — 'injecting a shared origin-guard header … the integration URI … injecting a static request-header parameter (`X-Opcert-Guard`) on the API Gateway integration'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC1: 'the test asserts the integration's `request_parameters` block includes `append:header.X-Opcert-Guard: "<value>"` (or the AWS mapping-expression equivalent) and cite the Terraform registry `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` `request_parameters` documentation confirming static literal values are supported in HTTP_PROXY integrations.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether the jq `.name` check targets the Terraform resource local label or the IAM policy `name` attribute (`.values.name`), and ensure the expected string matches the field being queried — e.g. change to `.[0].s.Sid == "opcert-admin-invoke"` (if a Sid is used) or `.values.name == "opcert-admin-invoke"` with a corrected jq path.
findings.finding: The AC1 structured test's jq assertion checks `.[0].name == "opcert-admin-invoke"` (hyphen-delimited), but in `terraform show -json` planned values the `.name` field for a resource is the Terraform local label, which by universal TF convention uses underscores (e.g. `opcert_admin_invoke`). If the implementer follows TF naming conventions the test will exit non-zero — not because of a security violation but because of a label-spelling mismatch — making the self-failing test fail for the wrong reason and undermining AC1's assurance value.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1, jq command: `.[0].name == "opcert-admin-invoke"`
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `{name: .name, …}` with `{name: .values.name, …}` in the jq pipeline (so `.name` refers to the AWS IAM policy's name attribute, which CAN be `opcert-admin-invoke` with hyphens), or add a `Sid` to the policy statement and check `.s.Sid == "OpcertAdminInvoke"` instead.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a live wrong-role rejection check to AC5: sign a request with a valid AWS identity that is NOT `rebar-opcert-admin` (e.g., the operator's user/role before assuming the admin role, or a second test IAM identity) and assert it is rejected (403) at the API Gateway, proving the `execute-api:Invoke` scope is enforced in the deployed environment, not just in the plan.
findings.finding: The live access-control checks in AC5 test only (a) a correctly-signed request from the authorized role and (b) a completely unsigned/unauthenticated request. They do NOT test a SigV4-signed request from a valid-but-wrong AWS identity (i.e., an identity that IS authenticated but is NOT the `rebar-opcert-admin` role). This failure path is the critical one for verifying that the `execute-api:Invoke` grant is correctly scoped to exactly the IaC-created role — a misconfigured trust policy or an overly-broad `execute-api:Invoke` could make the wrong-role request succeed while the plan-level jq check still passes (it checks plan intent, not deployed policy enforcement).
findings.location: AC5 (live checks) and AC1 (Terraform declarations)
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC5 with: 'a SigV4-signed request from a valid AWS identity that is NOT the rebar-opcert-admin role is rejected with 403 at API Gateway — evidence: captured response body/status alongside the invoking identity (aws sts get-caller-identity output).' This can be done by signing with the operator's base IAM user/role (before sts:AssumeRole to rebar-opcert-admin), which is already present in the authenticated AWS CLI session.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the ticket's `file_impact` metadata: replace `infra/opcert/` with `infra/terraform/` and remove the stale 'Fargate + Secrets Manager' rationale to match the plan body's actual targets.
findings.finding: The plan's file_impact metadata (ticket field) lists `infra/opcert/` as the Terraform target path, but the plan body consistently targets the existing `infra/terraform/` directory for all new HCL resources. No `infra/opcert/` directory exists or is described anywhere in the plan text — this is a stale/wrong metadata entry that could misdirect an executor scanning the ticket's file_impact field.
findings.location: Ticket file_impact metadata: {path: 'infra/opcert/', reason: 'Terraform for API GW + Fargate + Secrets Manager'}; plan What section (Terraform)
findings.suggested_fix: Update file_impact to: [{path: 'infra/terraform/', reason: 'extend existing module: API Gateway HTTP API, IAM role, SSM parameters, random provider'}, {path: 'infra/compose/docker-compose.yml', reason: 'add opcert service'}, {path: 'infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template', reason: 'add /opcert/ guarded route'}, {path: 'infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh', reason: 'new: materialize guard value from SSM'}, {path: '.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml', reason: 'pin deployed env public key'}]

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the leaf ticket's `file_impact` to replace `infra/opcert/` → `infra/terraform/` (reason: 'API Gateway HTTP API + IAM role + SSM SecureString parameters') and add `infra/compose/` (reason: 'opcert compose service + guarded nginx route'), removing the rejected Fargate/Secrets-Manager reference.
findings.finding: The leaf ticket's `file_impact` metadata declares `infra/opcert/` with the reason 'Terraform for API GW + Fargate + Secrets Manager' — both the target directory and the reason name resources (Fargate, Secrets Manager) that the parent's cost-verified runtime decision explicitly rejected and that the leaf plan body itself correctly excludes. This stale metadata constitutes a declared-scope artifact that contradicts the parent.
findings.location: Leaf ticket `file_impact` field: `{"path": "infra/opcert/", "reason": "Terraform for API GW + Fargate + Secrets Manager"}`
findings.suggested_fix: Set file_impact to: [{"path": "infra/terraform/", "reason": "API Gateway HTTP API v2 + rebar-opcert-admin IAM role + SSM SecureString parameters"}, {"path": "infra/compose/", "reason": "opcert compose service + guarded nginx route + materialize script"}, {"path": ".rebar/trusted_environments.yaml", "reason": "pin deployed env public key"}]

findings.finding: AC1's structured jq assertion verifies only the permission policy (that `execute-api:Invoke` appears in exactly one policy whose name is the admin-role policy), but does NOT structurally assert the trust policy — i.e., that the `aws_iam_role` resource's assume-role policy is restricted to `var.opcert_admin_principal_arns`. A passing jq result is compatible with a wide-open trust policy (e.g., trusting all AWS principals), leaving the core security invariant — 'a key nobody else can read' / access restricted to declared principals — without an end-state-observable proof.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1, structured jq assertion block
findings.suggested_fix:
Extend AC1 with a second jq command, e.g.:
```
terraform show -json tf.plan | jq '[.planned_values.root_module.resources[] | select(.type == "aws_iam_role" and .name == "rebar-opcert-admin") | .values.assume_role_policy | fromjson | .Statement[] | .Principal]'
```
and assert the result contains only the expected ARN(s) with no wildcard.

findings.finding: AC1 references the trust-policy restriction to `opcert_admin_principal_arns` as a security property but provides no structured assertion to verify it — the only STRUCTURED test command (the `jq` pipeline in AC1) checks for `execute-api:Invoke` in IAM policies, not that the `rebar-opcert-admin` role's trust policy is actually scoped to `opcert_admin_principal_arns` and nothing else.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1, jq command block
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC1's structured assertion block: `terraform show -json tf.plan | jq '[.planned_values.root_module.resources[] | select(.type=="aws_iam_role" and .name=="rebar-opcert-admin") | .values.assume_role_policy | fromjson | .Statement[].Principal]'` — assert the result contains only the expected principals and has length equal to the number of entries in `opcert_admin_principal_arns`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either provide an explicit positive rationale for why `hashicorp/random` is preferable over the established operator-seeded pattern for the origin-guard secret (and address the state-exposure concern), or adopt the consistent pattern: generate the guard value out-of-band (`openssl rand -hex 32`), seed it into SSM with `aws ssm put-parameter`, and declare the SSM parameter with `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` (no new provider needed).
findings.finding: The `hashicorp/random` provider is introduced (a new provider dependency) solely to generate the origin-guard value via `random_password`. This stores the guard value in Terraform state (readable by anyone with state access via `terraform state show`). The codebase's consistent pattern for all 12 secret values is operator-seeded out-of-band with `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }`, never stored in Terraform state. No positive rationale is given for why `hashicorp/random` is preferable to the established operator-seeded pattern for this secret value.
findings.location: What section — Terraform, origin-guard provisioning path; 'the `hashicorp/random` provider is ADDED'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Cite and verify the exact `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` Terraform attribute and key syntax for injecting a static request header (e.g., `request_parameters = { "append:header.X-Opcert-Guard" = "$stageVariables.guard" }` or the equivalent literal-value form), and add a structured AC assertion that the plan contains the correct key format.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that API Gateway HTTP API can inject a static `X-Opcert-Guard` request header via the `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` resource, but neither the Terraform attribute name nor the required key format is cited or verified. The `request_parameters` attribute on `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` uses a non-obvious key syntax (e.g., `append:header.X-Opcert-Guard`) that differs from REST API mapping semantics; a wrong or missing key silently produces a successful `terraform apply` while never injecting the header — causing the origin-guard inner perimeter to be absent with no deploy-time signal. Zero prior art exists for `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` in `infra/terraform/`.
findings.location: What section — 'maps it as a static request-header parameter (`X-Opcert-Guard`) on the API Gateway integration'
findings.suggested_fix: In the IaC spec, name the exact HCL: `resource "aws_apigatewayv2_integration" … { request_parameters = { "append:header.X-Opcert-Guard" = random_password.opcert_guard.result } }` and add to AC1's jq/plan assertion a check that a `request_parameters` entry whose key matches `append:header.X-Opcert-Guard` is present on the integration resource.

findings.finding: The plan specifies that Terraform will inject the origin-guard value as a static `X-Opcert-Guard` request header on the `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` resource, but never cites the exact Terraform attribute name or its required key format. The `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` resource's `request_parameters` attribute uses a non-obvious key syntax (keys must be prefixed with `append:`, `overwrite:`, or `remove:`, e.g. `overwrite:header.X-Opcert-Guard`). No `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` resource exists anywhere in `infra/terraform/` (confirmed by search), so there is no codebase prior art to reference. A wrong or missing key prefix is silently accepted by `terraform apply` but the header is never injected — the inner origin-guard perimeter then silently does not enforce, with no deploy-time error signal.
findings.location: What section — 'maps it as a static request-header parameter (`X-Opcert-Guard`) on the API Gateway integration'; AC1 — 'the box-origin integration with the static `X-Opcert-Guard` request-header mapping'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section or AC1: 'Use `aws_apigatewayv2_integration.request_parameters = { "overwrite:header.X-Opcert-Guard" = random_password.opcert_guard.result }` (the `overwrite:` prefix is required by the AWS provider for static header injection on HTTP API integrations).'

findings.finding: The plan specifies 'injecting a static request-header parameter (`X-Opcert-Guard`) on the API Gateway integration' but never states the Terraform HCL syntax for this on an HTTP API v2 integration. The mechanism (`request_parameters` map on `aws_apigatewayv2_integration`) uses a specific key syntax (e.g. `"append:header.X-Opcert-Guard"`) that differs from REST API v1 mapping templates and is a new, unverified integration surface in this codebase. The plan neither names the attribute nor the key-format convention, leaving the executor to discover it.
findings.location: What section: 'the box-origin integration with the static `X-Opcert-Guard` request-header mapping'; AC1: 'the box-origin integration with the static `X-Opcert-Guard` request-header mapping'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default mechanism for supplying `opcert_admin_principal_arns` at deploy (e.g. 'persisted in `infra/terraform/terraform.tfvars` as `opcert_admin_principal_arns = ["arn:aws:iam::ACCOUNT:user/operator"]` — not committed; passed via `-var-file` or as a `-var` flag') so the deploy step is fully self-contained.
findings.finding: The deploy procedure (Deploy + prove section) says 'terraform apply' and mentions the variable `opcert_admin_principal_arns` (set at deploy to the operator's IAM principals) but does not specify how that variable value is supplied — no tfvars file path, no `-var` CLI flag example, and no statement of where this value is persisted for future applies. An executor must either guess or stop to ask.
findings.location: What section — variable `opcert_admin_principal_arns`; Deploy + prove section — `terraform apply` step
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Deploy + prove section: 'Pass the variable via `terraform apply -var="opcert_admin_principal_arns=[\"<operator-ARN>\"]"` (or store in a gitignored `infra/terraform/operator.auto.tfvars`); record the value used in the operator-attested comment alongside the API endpoint outputs.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the ADR 0046 L4 citation in the Why section: state the actual rationale (zero fixed-cost constraint, ~$50+/mo new spend) directly without attributing it to L4, which addresses signing-key multi-party architecture, not infrastructure isolation.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that ADR 0046 L4 is the authority for rejecting the Fargate/NLB/Secrets-Manager/KMS-CMK stack ('a dedicated Fargate/NLB/Secrets-Manager/KMS-CMK stack was rejected (~$50+/mo new spend for isolation ADR 0046 L4 does not require'). A read of ADR 0046 L4 shows it is titled 'Single controlled certification environment; no threshold / multi-party signing' and addresses signing-key multi-party requirements — it says nothing about infrastructure isolation tiers (Fargate vs. EC2), VPC links, or cost of NLB/Secrets Manager. The actual rejection rationale is cost, not L4.
findings.location: Why section: 'a dedicated Fargate/NLB/Secrets-Manager/KMS-CMK stack was rejected (~$50+/mo new spend for isolation ADR 0046 L4 does not require)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'isolation ADR 0046 L4 does not require' with 'at a cost the zero-fixed-cost constraint prohibits' (or similar), keeping the cost rationale without the incorrect ADR attribution.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either provide the canonical schema for `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` (field names, required keys, format) inline in this plan, or add an explicit dependency on the epic story that establishes it, and ensure that story lands before this one.
findings.finding: The plan instructs pinning the deployed environment's public key and `env_id` in `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` 'per the epic's era protocol,' but this file does not exist anywhere in the repository — the `.rebar/` directory contains only a `cache/` subdirectory. No era-protocol schema, no existing entries, no format definition is visible in the codebase. This is an unverifiable assertion: the file and its schema are not established on `main`, so an executor has no concrete format to follow.
findings.location: What section — 'Deploy + prove': 'pin the environment's public key + env_id into .rebar/trusted_environments.yaml per the epic's era protocol'; AC4: 'The deployed environment's public key and env_id are pinned in .rebar/trusted_environments.yaml (with added_at_commit per the epic's era protocol)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` retries SSM reads with backoff (e.g., up to 3 attempts with exponential delay) and that `compose-up.sh` aborts (or at minimum logs a fatal error and skips `docker compose up`) if the materialize step exits non-zero, preventing the service from starting with an absent guard.
findings.finding: The materialize-opcert-guard.sh script reads from SSM at boot time with no retry/backoff or failure-mode specified. On a fresh box (IAM instance profile propagation lag, SSM endpoint momentarily unreachable, or throttling), a transient SSM failure will cause the script to abort, leaving /etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf absent or stale — but compose-up.sh will proceed to `docker compose up` anyway (the plan specifies no abort-on-failure gate), silently starting the service with a broken or missing guard header check.
findings.location: What section — infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh description; AC line 3 (compose-up.sh calls materialize before docker compose up)
findings.suggested_fix: Add `set -euo pipefail` to the materialize script and a retry loop (`for i in 1 2 3; do aws ssm get-parameter … && break || sleep $((i*5)); done || { echo 'FATAL: SSM unavailable'; exit 1; }`). In compose-up.sh, check the exit code before proceeding to `docker compose up`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback note for the nginx template change: e.g., revert the template commit, re-run compose-up.sh (or `nginx -t && nginx -s reload`), and confirm existing Gerrit routes respond — or note that the change is additive-only and document the `nginx -t` pre-flight check as part of the deploy procedure.
findings.finding: The plan modifies `infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template` — a live running host nginx config — by adding an `include` directive and a new `location` block, but specifies no rollback procedure if the template change causes the nginx reload on the live box to fail or inadvertently alters existing route behavior.
findings.location: What section — Compose/nginx wiring paragraph; AC line 3
findings.suggested_fix: Add `nginx -t` (config test) before `nginx -s reload` in the materialize script, so a malformed rendered config is caught before reload rather than silently failing.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify introducing `hashicorp/random` over the established operator-seeding pattern (`openssl rand -base64 32 | aws ssm put-parameter --overwrite`), or replace `random_password` with a placeholder + `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` matching the other 12 SecureStrings in `ssm.tf`.
findings.finding: `hashicorp/random` (`random_password`) is introduced as a new Terraform provider dependency with zero existing call-sites in `infra/terraform/` (Rule-of-Three: 0 of 3), while the codebase's established pattern for all 12 SSM SecureStrings in `ssm.tf` is operator-side seeding via `aws ssm put-parameter --overwrite` with `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }`. The single stated benefit of Terraform-managed generation — 'rotation = terraform apply -replace=' — is a hypothetical operational convenience, not a current done-definition requirement.
findings.location: What section, Terraform bullet: 'the `hashicorp/random` provider is ADDED to `infra/terraform/versions.tf` `required_providers`'
findings.suggested_fix: Declare `/rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard` the same way as the other 12 SecureStrings (value = 'CHANGEME', lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }); seed it out-of-band with `openssl rand -base64 32 | aws ssm put-parameter --name /rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard --type SecureString --overwrite --value -`; remove `hashicorp/random` from `required_providers`. The AC3 test for 'the Terraform integration URI uses the https:// scheme' already covers the integration-header wiring; the test for the guard value can stub SSM directly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the directory inconsistency: either move the new script to `infra/gerrit/materialize-opcert-guard.sh` (matching the cited `materialize-deploy-key.sh` / `materialize-g2p-config.sh` precedent) or explicitly acknowledge the divergence and update all references (compose-up.sh call site, AC3 test) to use `infra/scripts/`.
findings.finding: The plan cites `infra/gerrit/materialize-deploy-key.sh` and `infra/gerrit/materialize-g2p-config.sh` as the precedent for the new materialize script, but then places the new script at `infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh` — a different directory. Both confirmed precedent scripts live in `infra/gerrit/`, not `infra/scripts/`. This inconsistency means a reader or executor following the stated precedent would expect the new script in `infra/gerrit/`, and the `compose-up.sh` call site path would differ from what the precedent suggests.
findings.location: What section — Compose paragraph: 'the guard value also lands in the service env following the existing precedent (infra/gerrit/materialize-deploy-key.sh / materialize-g2p-config.sh — SSM → file)'
findings.suggested_fix: Locate the script at `infra/gerrit/materialize-opcert-guard.sh` to match the two confirmed precedents, or add a sentence explicitly stating the different directory choice and why.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh` as a consumer and add `infra/compose/Dockerfile.opcert` to `BOT_PATHS` (or a new `OPCERT_PATHS` trigger) so opcert container changes auto-deploy alongside review-bot changes.
findings.finding: infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh is an unenumerated consumer: its BOT_PATHS variable (line 55) lists 'infra/compose/Dockerfile.reviewbot' as a redeploy trigger but does NOT include 'infra/compose/Dockerfile.opcert'. After the opcert story lands, a change to Dockerfile.opcert on main will not trigger an auto-redeploy of the opcert service on the box — the auto-deployer will silently skip it.
findings.location: infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh line 55; plan Scope section / What section (Compose)
findings.suggested_fix: In autodeploy.sh line 55, extend BOT_PATHS to include 'infra/compose/Dockerfile.opcert', or add a parallel OPCERT_PATHS variable with the same changed-path-trigger logic used for BOT_PATHS.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the rationale: HTTP API v2 does support resource-based policies; restate the choice as a deliberate design decision (e.g., identity-policy-only is sufficient for the access control goal and simpler) rather than a capability absence.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'an HTTP API has no resource policy' as a factual constraint justifying the identity-policy-only design, but AWS API Gateway HTTP APIs (v2) do support resource-based policies. The claim is factually incorrect, and presenting it as a constraint gives a false rationale for the approach. The chosen approach (identity-policy on the IaC-created role) still works mechanically, but the stated justification is wrong.
findings.location: What section — Terraform, second bullet: 'Caller restriction is identity-policy-based (an HTTP API has no resource policy)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'an HTTP API has no resource policy' with 'identity-policy-only is sufficient: AWS_IAM on the routes enforces SigV4, and the IaC-created role with Invoke-scoped inline policy is the sole grant; a resource policy is available on HTTP APIs but adds complexity with no additional security benefit at this trust boundary.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify the introduction of `hashicorp/random` over the existing operator-seeded `aws_ssm_parameter` pattern (or Terraform's built-in `uuid()` function) — either document why Terraform-managed generation is preferred for the origin-guard but not for the 12 existing secrets, or adopt the established pattern and remove the new provider dependency.
findings.finding: The plan introduces `hashicorp/random` (`random_password`) as a new Terraform provider with zero existing call-sites in `infra/terraform/` to generate the origin-guard value, while the codebase's established prior art for all 12 secret/token parameters is operator-seeding into `aws_ssm_parameter` with `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` (no new provider required). Terraform's built-in `uuid()` function or `random_id` (which requires no provider addition in recent Terraform versions) are not considered. Introducing a new provider lock-file entry and `terraform init` refresh for a single call-site violates the Rule-of-Three and contradicts the existing pattern without justification.
findings.location: What section — 'the origin-guard header provisioning path: Terraform generates the guard value with `random_password` — the `hashicorp/random` provider is ADDED to `infra/terraform/versions.tf`'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `random_password` with Terraform's built-in `uuid()` function (no provider required, available since TF 1.x) to generate a stable per-workspace origin-guard value stored in `aws_ssm_parameter`, consistent with the existing module pattern and eliminating the new provider dependency. If rotation-via-replace is genuinely required, document it explicitly as the rationale for diverging from the operator-seeded pattern.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the claim 'an HTTP API has no resource policy' — HTTP API v2 does support resource-based policies via `aws_apigatewayv2_api.policy`; either update the plan to acknowledge this and explain why identity-policy-only is sufficient, or add a resource policy as a defense-in-depth layer.
findings.finding: The plan states 'an HTTP API has no resource policy' as the factual justification for using only identity-based IAM policy (no `aws_apigatewayv2_api` `policy` attribute in IaC). AWS API Gateway HTTP APIs (v2) do support resource-based policies — the `aws_apigatewayv2_api` resource exposes a `policy` attribute, and AWS documentation confirms HTTP APIs support resource policies for cross-account and IP-based restrictions. This is an unverified capability assertion that forecloses a defense-in-depth option without acknowledging the tradeoff.
findings.location: What section — 'identity-policy-based (an HTTP API has no resource policy): `AWS_IAM` auth requires SigV4'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'an HTTP API has no resource policy' with 'the plan relies on identity policy only (resource policies on HTTP APIs are available but not required here because the IaC-created role is the sole grant path and SigV4 enforcement is at the route level)' — or add an `aws_apigatewayv2_api` `policy` block restricting invocation to the `rebar-opcert-admin` role ARN as a second layer.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the claim 'an HTTP API has no resource policy' — HTTP API v2 does support resource policies; revise the sentence to accurately state the chosen design rationale (e.g., 'a resource policy is available but the identity-policy-only approach is sufficient for this threat model and avoids additional IaC surface').
findings.finding: The plan states 'an HTTP API has no resource policy' as the factual architectural premise for using identity-policy-only access control (no `aws_apigatewayv2_api` resource policy in IaC). This claim is contradicted by AWS capability: API Gateway HTTP APIs (v2) do support resource-based policies. The misstatement is load-bearing as a design justification — an implementer who accepts it will skip a valid defense-in-depth layer believing it is architecturally unavailable, and a future auditor may distrust the entire access-control rationale.
findings.location: What section — 'Caller restriction is identity-policy-based (an HTTP API has no resource policy)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'an HTTP API has no resource policy' with 'the identity-policy approach is sufficient — a resource policy is available on HTTP APIs but adds IaC surface without additional enforcement value given AWS_IAM already requires SigV4 from the IaC-created role'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the access posture for the Terraform state bucket (who may read it) as a security property of the origin-guard mechanism, OR add a defence-in-depth control that does not rely solely on state confidentiality (e.g., restrict the EC2 SG ingress on port 443 to the API Gateway IP ranges, or restrict the nginx origin guard path to a second network-level control), and document the chosen stance in the plan.
findings.finding: The origin-guard shared secret (random_password.result) lives in Terraform state as cleartext JSON (protected only by S3-level backend encryption), yet the plan never declares who may read the state bucket. Because the EC2 origin is publicly reachable on port 443 (SG opens 443 to 0.0.0.0/0), any principal with s3:GetObject on the state bucket can extract the guard value and bypass API Gateway/SigV4 entirely — calling the opcert origin directly with the guard header. The plan's access posture for this secret path is undeclared.
findings.location: What section — 'origin-guard header provisioning path' / infra/terraform/network.tf ingress rule 443 0.0.0.0/0 / AC1 (guard parameter, no lifecycle restriction mentioned for state access)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence in the 'origin-guard header provisioning path' section: 'State confidentiality: the Terraform state bucket's s3:GetObject is restricted to the operator roles listed in opcert_admin_principal_arns and the CI plan role — the guard value's secrecy depends on this. To eliminate the state-read dependency, the SG ingress on port 443 MAY be narrowed to API Gateway-managed IPs (or a VPC-confined NLB/VPC Link), but this is advisory for a single-tenant deployment.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR (or extend an existing one, e.g. ADR 0047) documenting the origin-guard pattern — its purpose, the Terraform→SSM→nginx→API-GW data flow, rotation procedure, and failure modes — so the security contract is discoverable from the repo after this story merges.
findings.finding: The origin-guard header pattern (Terraform `random_password` → SSM SecureString → nginx `map` block → API Gateway static request-header injection) is a novel security/operational contract that is described only in plan prose. No ADR or persistent design-doc update is planned by this story, leaving future operators and contributors without a durable record of why this pattern was chosen, how guard rotation works end-to-end, and what breaks if either side diverges.
findings.location: What section (origin-guard header provisioning path) and AC1/AC3
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/adr/0048-opcert-origin-guard-pattern.md` (or append a 'Origin-guard mechanism' section to the existing deploy ADR) covering: (1) threat model addressed, (2) the full data-flow (random_password → SSM → API GW static header / materialize-script → nginx map), (3) rotation runbook (`terraform apply -replace=random_password.opcert_guard` + nginx reload), and (4) what a misconfiguration looks like. Reference it from a comment in the Terraform integration resource.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at minimum: (a) a CloudWatch log group + `access_log_settings` on the API Gateway stage for request-level visibility, and (b) a `restart: unless-stopped` (or equivalent) policy and a `healthcheck` on the `opcert` compose service entry — or explicitly document why these are deferred.
findings.finding: No observability is specified for the new API Gateway endpoint or the opcert compose service — no CloudWatch access logs on the HTTP API, no container-level log routing, no metric alarms for 4xx/5xx rates or latency. The operator has no instrumented signal when the new failure point (API GW → nginx → opcert container) is degraded.
findings.location: What section — Terraform API Gateway declarations; Compose service definition

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a key-rotation runbook (even a brief AC note or inline comment): the steps to generate a new Ed25519 key, overwrite the SSM SecureString via `aws ssm put-parameter --overwrite`, redeploy the compose service, and re-pin the new public key in `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` — and specify what happens to in-flight attestations signed by the old key.
findings.finding: The Ed25519 private key (`/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key`) is shared state with `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` — meaning Terraform can never update it after the initial placeholder is written. The plan defines no key-rotation procedure: no operator runbook, no `aws ssm put-parameter --overwrite` command, no guidance on how to handle a compromised key. This is shared state consumed by the live signing service with an undefined UPDATE and RETIRE lifecycle.
findings.location: What section — Terraform SSM parameter declarations; 'Deploy + prove' key seeding step

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `REBAR_OPCERT_ENV_ID` must be a distinct config key from the existing `REBAR_ENV_ID` / `.env-id` mechanism, and if so, explain in the plan why the opcert service's cryptographic identity requires a separate namespace rather than reusing the established env_id surface.
findings.finding: `REBAR_OPCERT_ENV_ID` introduces a new compose-service env-var config surface for environment identity while the codebase already has `REBAR_ENV_ID` (documented in `docs/env-vars.md`, widely used in tests and the reconciler) and `_seam.env_id()` reading `.env-id` for the same concept. The plan does not explain why the opcert service needs a distinct env-var rather than reusing the existing identity mechanism.
findings.location: What section, Compose bullet: 'an `opcert` service … with env `REBAR_OPCERT_ENV_ID`'; AC3: 'the env wiring (`REBAR_OPCERT_ENV_ID`, parameter names)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify at minimum: (a) a CloudWatch log group and access log configuration for the API Gateway stage, and (b) a `logging: driver: journald` entry for the opcert compose service — and state whether a CloudWatch alarm for integration errors is in or out of scope with a rationale.
findings.finding: The plan adds a new internet-reachable API Gateway endpoint and a new `opcert` compose service but specifies no CloudWatch logging, metrics, or alarms for either. The existing monitoring stack (monitoring.tf, monitoring_s5.tf, etc.) is not extended. There is no specified log group for API Gateway access logs, no alarm for integration errors or high 4xx/5xx rates on the opcert routes, and no observability for the opcert container itself.
findings.location: What section — 'NO new fixed-monthly-cost resources'; Acceptance Criteria (none mention observability for APIGW or opcert service); Scope — 'In: the Terraform extension, compose/nginx wiring…'

findings.finding: The jq proving command in AC1 produces an array but never asserts its cardinality or the name value inline — the plan prose says 'asserting the resulting array has length 1 and its name is the rebar-opcert-admin invoke policy' but the literal command shown omits that assertion step. A CI step running the command as written would silently pass even if the array were empty or contained unexpected entries, satisfying no guarantee.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1, jq command block
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace the bare jq expression with:
```bash
terraform plan -out=tf.plan && terraform show -json tf.plan \
  | jq -e '
    [ .planned_values.root_module.resources[]
      | select(.type == "aws_iam_role_policy" or .type == "aws_iam_policy")
      | {name: .name, s: (.values.policy | fromjson | .Statement[])}
      | select([.s.Action] | flatten | any(. == "execute-api:Invoke"))
    ]
    | if length == 1 and .[0].name == "rebar-opcert-admin-invoke-policy"
      then .
      else error("invoke-grant assertion failed: \(.)")
      end
  '
```
This makes the command exit 1 (and print the offending array) on any deviation from the expected single-role constraint.

findings.finding: AC1's proving command for IAM exclusivity ('ONLY statement with action execute-api:Invoke is the one attached to rebar-opcert-admin') is described only in prose. No concrete jq expression is given to (a) extract all IAM statements with that action from the tf.plan JSON and (b) assert their count is exactly 1. A test that checks existence of the correct statement but omits the cardinality check would pass even if a second Invoke-granting statement were introduced, making the security invariant unverifiable.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC1 — structured assertion block
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC1: 'The cardinality assertion uses `jq -e` on the structured plan JSON: `terraform show -json tf.plan | jq -e \"[.. | objects | select(.actions? and (.actions[] | contains(\\"execute-api:Invoke\\"))) ] | length == 1\"` — this MUST return exit 0; any additional Invoke statement causes exit 1.'

findings.finding: AC1 asserts 'no other principal is granted [execute-api:Invoke]' but provides no proving command or test approach for this negative assertion. Asserting an absence across all IAM policy documents in the Terraform configuration (inline policies, resource-based policies, any future additions) requires exhaustive scanning, not just verifying the one IaC-created role. With no described approach, an executor has no guidance on how to implement this check, and a test that only verifies the `rebar-opcert-admin` role's inline policy exists would silently pass even if a second policy inadvertently also granted Invoke.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC1: 'A conftest (or equivalent) policy test scans all `aws_iam_role_policy`, `aws_iam_policy_document`, and `aws_iam_role` resources in `infra/terraform/` and asserts that `execute-api:Invoke` appears only in the `rebar-opcert-admin` inline policy; the test fails if any other resource document contains this action.'

findings.finding: The plan asserts a 'HOST-nginx conf include' mechanism for the origin-guard value, but infra/nginx/ contains only rebar.conf.template with no include directive and no /opcert/ location block. The nginx conf include target, the include directive wiring it into the live server config, and the /opcert/ location block must all be added to rebar.conf.template (or a new file + include stanza) — but the plan never identifies rebar.conf.template as the file to modify or specifies where the include directive is inserted, leaving the 'HOST-nginx conf include' claim unbacked by the existing nginx configuration.
findings.location: What section / Compose — nginx wiring paragraph: 'nginx route /opcert/ → the service that rejects requests whose X-Opcert-Guard header does not match … materialized from its SSM parameter into a HOST-nginx conf include'
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan's Compose/nginx wiring paragraph, replace 'a HOST-nginx conf include' with a concrete specification: the materialize script writes the guard value to `/etc/nginx/conf.d/opcert-guard.conf` (a `map` or `geo` block, or a simple variable assignment), `rebar.conf.template` is updated to add an `include /etc/nginx/conf.d/opcert-guard.conf;` directive and a new `location /opcert/ { … if ($http_x_opcert_guard != $opcert_guard_value) { return 403; } … }` block. AC3 should name these two files as the test targets.

findings.finding: The origin-guard SSM parameter (/rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard) is given lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] } but its value is Terraform-managed (sourced from random_password.result, not operator-seeded). This means if random_password is ever replaced (state taint, state loss, provider upgrade), Terraform updates the API Gateway static header to the new guard value but leaves the SSM parameter frozen at the old value. The materialize script reads from SSM, so nginx enforces the stale guard while API Gateway injects the new one — breaking every request until a manual SSM fix.
findings.location: What section — origin-guard provisioning path; also AC1 ('the two SecureString parameters ... guarded by lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }')
findings.suggested_fix: Do not add lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] } to the /rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard SSM parameter. Only the Ed25519 key parameter (whose value is operator-seeded out-of-band) needs that guard. The guard SSM parameter's value should always track random_password.result so APIGW and nginx stay in sync. Separately, document that rotating the guard requires both a terraform apply (updates APIGW header) and re-running materialize-opcert-guard.sh (updates nginx).

findings.finding: infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template is an unenumerated consumer: the plan introduces a new nginx conf include for the /opcert/ route with X-Opcert-Guard enforcement, but the existing template has no `include` directive and is never named in the plan's What or Scope sections as requiring modification.
findings.location: What section — 'Compose — extend infra/compose/docker-compose.yml' / 'a HOST-nginx conf include'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
